title: The Impact of Salience on Investor Behavior: Evidence from a Natural Experiment
abstract: We test whether the display of information causally affects investor behavior in a high-stakes trading environment. Using investor-level brokerage data from China and a natural experiment, we estimate the impact of a shock that increased the salience of a stock's purchase price but did not change the investor's information set. We employ a difference-in-differences approach and find that the salience shock causally increased the disposition effect by 17%. We use microdata to document substantial heterogeneity across investors in the treatment effect. A previously documented trading pattern, the “rank effect,” explains heterogeneity in the change in the disposition effect.

article.introduction:
In most models of investorbehavior, the manner in which information is displayed has little impact on portfolio choice. Yet, evidence from several laboratory experiments indicates that information display can substantially affect investor behavior. For example, manipulating the presentation of an asset's past performance can affect subjects’ risk-taking (Gneezy and Potters (1997), Thaler et al. (1997)), mutual fund choice (Choi, Madrian, and Laibson (2010)), and willingness to sell losing stocks (Frydman and Rangel (2014)).
Outside the laboratory, it is less clear whether a change in information display would also have a large effect on trading decisions. One recent experiment finds that when subjects make decisions over the course of a calendar year—compared to a single laboratory session—the effect of information display on portfolio risk-taking largely disappears (Beshears et al. (2017)). This result highlights the importance of the experimental setting. Thus, there is a need for additional empirical evidence from a higher-stakes and more natural trading environment.
In this paper, we use a combination of microdata from a Chinese brokerage house and a natural experiment to estimate the causal effect of a change in information display on individual investor trading decisions. Specifically, we obtain data from a brokerage house that increased the salience of a stock's capital gain by making it more visually prominent on the investor's online trading screen (Figure1). To assess the impact on investor behavior, we measure the disposition effect, which is the greater tendency to sell stocks with capital gains compared to capital losses (Shefrin and Statman (1985), Odean (1998)).1We hypothesize that increasing the salience of a stock's capital gain will increase the disposition effect.
In general, there are three main challenges in estimating the causal effect of information display on investor behavior in the field. First, in many circumstances, the display of information is correlated with the information itself. For example, newspapers and online brokerages often prominently display a list of daily winner and loser stocks, which confounds the content of information (the daily return) with the salience of information (its display on the list) (though see Kaniel and Parham (2017) and Wang (2019)). Here, we use a unique shock to the display of information about a stock's capital gain that is uncorrelated with the capital gain itself. Because information on the capital gain was available to investors before the change in information display, this did not affect the information content of the capital gain, but rather made it more salient and easier for the investor to process. We therefore refer to the change as a “salience shock” rather than an information shock.
Second, estimating the effect on theaverageinvestor's behavior can mask important heterogeneity that makes the size of the effect hard to interpret. In particular, investors who hold many stocks in their portfolio may exhibit greater changes in behavior because more information becomes prominently displayed on their trading screen. This could lead the average effect to be overestimated if larger investors contribute more observations to the analysis than smaller investors. Our data provide information on trades at the account level, and thus we can estimate the effect at the individual level (Taubinsky and Rees-Jones (2018)).
The third challenge is that the change in display may coincide with a distinct but unobserved shock that also affects trading behavior. The salience shock that we study generated a natural experiment that provides a control group, which we use to control for common time-series variation in the disposition effect. In particular, our data provide information on the method of trade—internet, phone, or in-person (Barber and Odean (2002)). Because the change in information display occurred online, the salience shock should not affect investors who traded by phone or in-person. Thus, we can employ a difference-in-differences approach to estimate the causal effect of the salience shock.
The main result in our paper is that after the salience shock, there is a significant increase in the disposition effect among internet investors (treatment group), compared to noninternet investors (control group). We show that the two groups have parallel trends before the salience shock and thus we can interpret the change in display as causally affecting trading behavior. Moreover, the change among the treatment group is sizable: The disposition effect increases by 17%, relative to the control group. This represents both an economically and a statistically meaningful change in trading behavior that takes place in a high-stakes environment. The average portfolio size among internet investors in our sample is 63,916 RMB, and annual disposable income per capita of citizens in China during our sample period is approximately 10,000 RMB.
While the average investor exhibits a significant increase in the disposition effect, we also document substantial cross-investor variation in the size of this change. Some investors exhibit a very large increase in the disposition effect, while others exhibit no change at all. We then test whether a distinct, but potentially related, trading bias can explain a part of this variation. This analysis is useful because documenting a correlation with a previously observed trading pattern can provide insight into a common mechanism that drives distinct behavioral effects (Chapman et al. (2018)).
Empirically, we find that variation in the change in the disposition effect can be explained by a previously documented trading bias called the “rank effect” (Hartzmark (2015)). This trading pattern refers to an investor's tendency to sell the extreme ranked stocks in his or her portfolio. Hartzmark (2015) provides some evidence that the rank effect is driven by a combination of salience and investor sophistication, which are also plausible explanations for the change in the disposition effect that we document here. We estimate the rank effect at the investor level in two ways: one based on ranking by returns and one based on ranking by alphabetical company name. Regardless of how we measure the rank effect, we find that it is significant in our sample, and that it correlates across investors with the change in the disposition effect after the salience shock.
While the natural experiment that we analyze is useful in assessing causality, our data do not enable us to identify the mechanism that generates our results. The change in trading behavior that we document is likely driven by a combination of several mechanisms. First, investors may focus their attention on attributes that are salient, and thus attach more weight to the capital gain variable after it becomes prominently displayed (Fehr and Rangel (2011), Bhatia (2013)). Second, our results may be driven by a bounded rationality mechanism, whereby investors optimally trade off the cost of acquiring information about the capital gain (three “mouse clicks” in our case) against the benefit of using this information in their decision (Caplin and Dean (2015), Gabaix (2019)). Third, investors may perceive the addition of the capital gain variable to the trading screen as advice from the brokerage company (Benartzi (2001), Madrian and Shea (2001)). After presenting our empirical results, we discuss these potential mechanisms in greater detail.
While the main focus of our paper is the trading response to the salience shock, our results also contribute to the literature on the disposition effect. One strand of this literature proposes formal models to better understand the mechanism that generates the disposition effect (Barberis and Xiong (2009,2012), Ingersoll and Jin (2013), Meng and Weng (2017)); another strand provides empirical tests of these models (Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), Frydman et al. (2014), Birru (2015), Chang, Solomon, and Westerfield (2016), Imas (2016), Heimer (2016), An et al. (2019)).2While the cause of the disposition effect is still debated, we provide evidence that the salience of information interacts with the underlying mechanism.3In addition, our results from the field complement those from a recent set of lab experiments that provide procedures for causally decreasing the disposition effect (Frydman and Rangel (2014), Fischbacher, Hoffmann, and Schudy (2017), Frydman, Hartzmark, and Solomon (2018)).
We also contribute to a broader empirical literature that measures the effect of information salience and disclosure on decision making (Loewenstein, Sunstein, and Golman (2014)). In a consumer choice environment, shoppers respond more strongly to sales tax when it is salient and included in the posted price, compared to when it is added at the register (Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009), Taubinsky and Rees-Jones (2018)). In a setting related to ours, Shaton (2017) finds that changing the salience of past mutual fund returns leads to a change in aggregate market outcomes.4Choi et al. (2017) demonstrate that providing small cues that make a high savings rate salient lead to an increase in 401k contributions. Badoer, Costello, and James (2019) find that a more prominent disclosure of the indirect fees earned by 401(k) service providers is associated with a reduction in total fees paid by smaller retirement plans. We add to this literature by providing evidence that the salience of information causally affects individual trading decisions in a high-stakes environment.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the data and the natural experiment. SectionIIpresents results from our difference-in-differences estimation. SectionIIIdescribes results on heterogeneity across investors. SectionIVdiscusses potential mechanisms and SectionVprovides robustness checks. SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Data and Natural Experiment:
We use an account-level data set to investigate trading behavior among investors at a security brokerage company in the People's Republic of China.5Our data set is very similar to the Chinese data set used by Feng and Seasholes (2004,2005), as well as to the U.S. data set used by Odean (1998). Our data come from a brokerage company that has multiple branches throughout China and serves approximately half a million investors in total. The main data set comes from one of these branches and consists of three files: a trade file, a position file, and an investor demographic file. Our sample period is from January 2003 through December 2009. The trade file provides data at the account-date-stock level and contains the trade time (hour: minute: second), stock ticker, buy/sell indicator, transaction price, number of shares purchased/sold, trading method (phone, internet, in-person), commissions, and taxes. We restrict our analyses to trading of common stocks, and there are no short sales in our data since short selling was prohibited in China during our sample period.
A key variable used in our empirical methodology is the trading method. An investor can place an order online, over the phone, or in-person at their home branch of the brokerage company. Our main empirical analysis focuses on one branch of this brokerage company because we only have trading method data for this one branch. Securities law in China during our sample period prohibits investors from holding more than one account, and requires that each investor conduct all trades via the branch he or she opened the account with. This means that for every investor in our data set, we observe all trades for that investor during our sample period. Chinese investors typically open an account close to where they live (Feng and Seasholes (2004)), but if they are traveling to a different city and want to place an order, they can do so through the phone or the internet.
When a trade is executed, both sellers and buyers need to pay a set of fees.6The most important feature of the fee structure in our setting is that there is no capital gains tax. While the sale of a position will trigger a tax bill, the tax base is the amount of the entire position, not the capital gain or loss. In other words, selling both winners and losers in China during our sample period will generate a tax bill, and hence we can rule out tax-loss selling as an explanation for our results.
In October 2004, one branch of the brokerage company for which we have trading method data altered the online display of their client's portfolio information. We refer to this branch as the “main branch.” Before October 2004, when an online investor from the main branch logged in to the online trading platform, the software displayed information about each of the investor's stock positions. The variables displayed for each stock included stock name, stock ticker, number of shares held, current price, current value (current price times number of shares held), non-tradable shares, and two other variables related to frozen shares.7
On October 1, 2004, the main branch added five variables to the investor's portfolio page: (I) weighted-average purchase price, (II) break-even price, (III) realized gain/loss, (IV) paper gain/loss, and (V) total gain/loss. The calculation of the weighted average purchase price does not consider commissions, taxes, or cash dividends, but does take stock dividends and stock splits into account. The break-even price, the market price at which liquidating the entire (remaining) position would make the complete round-trip trade (since the initial purchase) break even, accounts for commissions, taxes, cash dividends, stock dividends, and stock splits. The realized gain/loss is equal to#shares sold× (sale price–weighted average purchase price) +cash dividendif there has been a partial sale or a cash dividend, and zero otherwise. The paper gain/loss is equal to#shares held× (current price–weighted average purchase price). Finally, the total gain/loss is the sum of the realized gain/loss and the paper gain/loss. In addition to the inclusion of these variables, the font color changed: Before October 2004, the font of each position was blue, whereas after the change, those positions trading at a gain were shown in red font while those positions trading at a loss were displayed in blue font. Figure1shows the trading screen before and after the change in information display.
Before the display change, each investor had access to his own transaction history. Together with the current price, which was displayed on the homepage for each stock the investor owned (before and after the shock), this information is sufficient to compute all five variables that were added to the portfolio homepage. Hence, the change in information display made these variables easier to process, but it did not affect their information content.
Note that the five variables that were added are all closely related to one another. With the weighted-average purchase price, the computation of realized and paper gains is straightforward. The weighted-average purchase price and break-even variables are highly correlated and very close to each other in most cases; these two variables typically differ only when there have been partial sales with significant realized gains or losses. Perhaps the more important difference is that the term “break-even” may lead some investors to think that selling above the break-even price is the “right” thing to do. Later in the paper, we address this concern by testing whether selling behavior is more sensitive to the weighted-average purchase price compared to the break-even price.
The change in information display was not driven by pressure from investors nor was it driven by a regulatory motive. In discussions with representatives from the brokerage company, we learned that the primary reason for the display change was to improve clients’ access to their account information, in line with competing brokerage houses. The change was not considered an important event and no pilot programs were conducted. Investors who traded online prior to October 1, 2004, received a message the first time they logged onto the trading platform after the display change alerting them to the improved access to their account information. Those traders who did not trade online before October 2004 but who subsequently moved online did not receive an explicit message about the change in information display.
Our main methodology follows Feng and Seasholes (2005), Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), and Barber and Odean (2013). Specifically, we use the transaction data set and the position data set to construct a holding sample at the investor-stock-day level. Investor-days on which an investor does not trade are included in the analysis. Purchases and sales are aggregated on a daily basis for each investor-stock. For example, if an investor bought Sinopec stock on January 2, 2003, and held it until January 29, 2003, there will be 19 observations (19 business days).8
We flag those days on which a position is opened or when shares are sold (including partial sales). We exclude positions for which we do not have information on the purchase price, which occurs mainly because investors bought their stocks before the start of our sample period. For an investor to be included in our analysis, we require that his position data be nonmissing in both the preperiod and the postperiod. We also require that he traded at least once in the preperiod, so that we have information on his trading method.
We define an “internet investor” as an investor who placed at least one order via the internet before October 2004, while a “noninternet investor” is an investor who never traded online before October 2004. We use data from before October 2004 to define these groups to avoid potential selection issues whereby the investor endogenously chooses his trading method in the postperiod based on the change in information display.9TableI, Panel A, provides summary statistics using this definition. We find that there is a roughly even split between the two groups. Among the internet investor group, approximately two-thirds place orders exclusively through the internet, while one-third use a combination of internet and other methods.
Among the noninternet investor group, most investors refrain from using the internet to place trades even after the salience shock. Specifically, among noninternet investors, only 9.2% of trades in the postperiod were placed through the internet. TableI, Panel B, provides summary statistics on investor characteristics for each of the two groups. The table shows that there are clear differences between the two groups, as internet investors hold portfolios with larger size and more stocks. Internet investors trade more frequently (as indicated by the higher unconditional selling propensity) and are also slightly older.10When presenting our main results in the next section, we conduct a matching exercise that shows that our results are not sensitive to these differences across groups.
article.II. Estimating the Causal Effect of Info:
In this section, we describe our empirical strategy and present our main result. To test for a causal effect of the salience shock on the disposition effect, we use a difference-in-differences methodology. Because the change in information display should affect only those investors who trade online, our treatment group comprises the set of internet investors and our control group the set of noninternet investors (those who trade on the phone or in-person at their designated branch). The change in information display occurs on October 1, 2004. The preperiod therefore runs from the beginning of our sample, January 1, 2003, through September 30, 2004. In our primary analysis, we define the postperiod as October 1, 2004, through June 30, 2006, to balance the lengths of the preperiod and postperiod at 21 months. Later in the paper, we check whether our main result is robust to including the full sample from January 2003 through December 2009.
Columns (1) and (2) of TableIIreport the estimation results for internet and noninternet investors, respectively. For presentation purposes, all coefficients are multiplied by 100. The coefficient on theGaindummy for both groups is strongly positive, indicating that, in the preperiod, both groups exhibit a significant disposition effect. In the preperiod, the probability that an internet investor sells a winning stock is 7.4% compared to 2.5% for a losing stock. Noninternet investors exhibit a similar disposition effect, as they sell winning stocks with a probability of 5.5% compared to 1.5% for losing stocks. Moreover, the coefficient onGain×Postis significantly positive for internet investors but is not statistically different from zero for noninternet investors, which indicates that internet investors exhibit a significant increase in the disposition effect after the salience shock but noninternet investors do not. Column (3) reports the results for the full model estimation. We find that the coefficient on the key triple interaction variable is significantly positive.
Columns (4) to (6) add several control variables known to affect selling propensities (Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012)). Specifically, we add controls for the holding period, weighted-average purchase price, volatility (depending on sign of return), returns, and the interaction between returns and holding period. The control variables are defined as follows:TimeOwned, the square root of the number of days since the position has been open;log(buyprice), the natural logarithm of the weighted-average purchase price;Volatility−, stock volatility calculated using the previous 250 days’ daily returns if the return since purchase is negative, and zero otherwise;Volatility+, stock volatility calculated using the previous 250 days’ daily returns if the return since purchase is positive, and zero otherwise;Return−, the return since purchase if it is negative, and zero otherwise; andReturn+, the return since purchase if it is positive, and zero otherwise. Consistent with previous results using U.S. data, we find that longer holding periods are associated with lower selling propensities while volatility is associated with higher selling propensities (Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012)). After including these controls, the magnitude of the coefficient on the key triple interaction becomes larger and thet-statistic becomes stronger.
The increase in the disposition effect among internet investors is economically large. In the analysis without any controls, the difference-in-differences estimate is 0.85%, which is 17.5%(0.85%4.86%)of the baseline disposition effect among internet investors in the preperiod. The difference-in-differences estimate increases to 1.54% with the set of full controls, which represents an increase of more than a quarter of the level of the preperiod disposition effect (6.09%). In TableIII, we reestimate equation1including bothInvestor×GainandInvestor×Dayfixed effects. These control for individual-level heterogeneity in the disposition effect and day-specific selling propensities, respectively. We find that the coefficient on the triple interaction remains significant after including these fixed effects.
Figure2plots these coefficients, along with the 95% confidence intervals, where standard errors are clustered by both day and investor. Visual inspection shows a divergence in the two time series immediately following the salience shock in the third quarter of 2004.
Time series of the average disposition effect. The figure displays the average disposition effect of the internet and noninternet investors in the main branch, estimated from the equation

To investigate this more formally, we run a multiple placebo test analysis. We first reduce the length of each preperiod and postperiod to one quarter each. Then, for each pair of adjacent quarters in our full sample (2003Q1 through 2009Q4), we define the placebo date as the end of the first quarter in the pair and we estimate the difference-in-differences. For example, for the first pair of adjacent quarters in our sample, 2003Q1 and 2003Q2, we define the placebo event date to be the end of 2003Q1. We then estimate the regression in1for each of the 27 pairs of adjacent quarters and we record thet-statistic on the triple interactionGain×Post×Internetfrom each regression (see Heimer (2016) for a similar analysis). Figure3plots the time series oft-statistics. The figure shows a spike in thet-statistic in the period that coincides with the “actual” treatment date (highlighted with horizontal lines)—this is the only regression for which we find the coefficient on the triple interaction to be significant at the 5% level.
In looking at Figure2, one potential concern is that the divergence in the disposition effects between the two groups is driven mainly by a decrease in the disposition effect among the control group. While this does not technically violate the validity of the difference-in-differences estimator, it raises the concern that there may be a group-specific shock to the disposition effect that coincides with the change in information display. To address this concern, we obtain data from two additional branches to examine whether there was a similar drop in the disposition effect among investors at these branches.
The second branch for which we obtain data is from the same brokerage firm and the same city as our main branch. For this second branch, we have data on 4,518 investors that satisfy the same data requirements as those used for our main branch. We do not have information on whether these investors traded via phone or internet, but we do know that purchase price and capital gain information was displayed on the trading screen to investors in the second branch throughout our full sample period.11In other words, the trading platform for our main branch after the salience shock is identical to the trading platform for the second branch over the full sample period.
We estimate the time series of the disposition effect for the second branch using the model in2. We plot the resulting coefficient estimates in the upper panel of Figure4, Panel A, along with the previously estimated time series for the two groups in our main branch. The figure shows that there is a clear decrease in the disposition effect among investors in the second branch at the time of the salience shock in October 2004. We purge this common component from investors in our main branch by subtracting the disposition effect of investors from the second branch. The lower panel of Figure4, Panel A, provides the two time series from our main branch after this subtraction, where the vertical axis now provides the disposition effect for the treatment and control groups relative to the disposition effect in the second branch. We see no significant difference between the two groups until immediately following the salience shock, when the effect is driven mainly by an increase in the treatment group. We also find that our difference-in-differences estimate remains significant when using the second branch as a control group (see TableIA.III).
Data on two additional branches—the second branch. The upper panel displays the average disposition effect across all investors from the second branch, estimated from the equation

In an effort to address group-specific shocks to internet investors, we obtain data from a third branch that do contain information on the method of trade, but that come from a different brokerage. For this third branch, we have data on the trading records of 7,542 investors (3,995 internet investors and 3,547 noninternet investors) over the period 2003Q1 to 2005Q4. Like our second branch, this branch displayed capital gain information on the investor's online trading screen over this sample period. The investors in this third branch are very similar to those in our main branch (see TableIA.IVfor summary statistics).
The upper panel of Figure4, Panel B, plots the time series of the disposition effect for internet and noninternet investors from our main branch and the third branch. We see that there is a decrease in the disposition effect for both internet and noninternet investors in the third branch around the salience shock, similar to the change for noninternet investors from our main branch. In the lower panel of Figure4, Panel B, we plot the difference between branches, again split by internet and noninternet investors. This essentially provides a triple difference, and we see an increase in the internet group after the salience shock. We are cautious, however, in interpreting the internet investors from the third branch as a control group, as the trends before the salience shock are not parallel between the two internet groups. With this caution in mind, TableIA.Vpresents formal regression results from the difference-in-differences test using the third branch as a control group.
These analyses indicate that all groups, except for internet investors at the main branch, exhibit a decrease in the disposition effect immediately following the treatment date. In particular, the decrease in the disposition effect among noninternet investors at our main branch appears to be sizable (Figure2). However, if we extend our sample to 2009, the magnitude of the decrease in 2004Q4 is relatively mild compared to the 2007 to 2009 period (FigureIA.2). This raises the important question of what drives the substantial time-series variation, including the common decrease among control groups after the salience shock. In several models of the disposition effect, trading depends on time-varying parameters, and thus the disposition effect can vary over time. For example, in models of realization utility, the disposition effect depends on the volatility and expected return of a stock, as well as on the investor's sensitivity to losses (Barberis and Xiong (2012), Ingersoll and Jin (2013)). Other potential explanations rely on an investor's beliefs and reference point, which are both likely to vary over time (Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), Birru (2015), Meng and Weng (2017)).12While we include a control for volatility in our regressions, some of these other parameters are not directly observable. We speculate that the fluctuation in the average investor's disposition effect may be driven by the aggregation of these time-varying stock-level and investor-level variables.
In summary, the data from the two additional branches provide some evidence that mitigates the concern of unobserved group-specific shocks. While neither branch serves as the perfect control group, the second branch helps address brokerage-specific shocks while the third branch helps address internet investor–specific shocks.
While the trends before the salience shock in Figure2appear to be parallel, TableIdemonstrates that there are differences in observable characteristics between the internet and noninternet groups in our main branch. Relative to noninternet investors, internet investors have larger portfolios, hold more stocks, and most importantly, trade more frequently. To help reduce concerns that our results may be confounded by these systematic differences, we perform a matching exercise where we match investors on these characteristics. In addition, given our focus on the disposition effect, we also match on the disposition effect in the preperiod.
Of the 1,754 internet investors, 502 cannot be matched to any eligible control investor within standard tolerances (specifically, a 0.005 caliper). The final matched sample therefore consists of 1,252 internet investors and a corresponding sample of 1,252 noninternet investors matched on investor characteristics measured in the preperiod. We estimate equation1based on this matched sample. TableIVshows that the coefficient on the key triple interactionGain×Post×Internet, without controls in columns (1) to (3), and with controls in columns (4) to (6), is significantly positive. The magnitudes are also similar to those in TableII. Figure5displays a graphical test of the parallel trends assumption among the matched sample. We see that in this matched sample, there continues to be no significant difference in the disposition effect trends between the two groups of investors.13
Time series of the average disposition effect—the propensity-score-matched sample. The figure displays estimation results from the following equation based on the propensity score–matched sample

Thus far, we have measured the disposition effect as the difference between the propensity to sell winning stocks and the propensity to sell losing stocks. One concern with this measure is that it can fluctuate over time with portfolio turnover (Feng and Seasholes (2005)). In particular, if overall turnover increases after the change in information display, our previous analysis would regard this as an increase in the disposition effect. The matching analysis in the previous section partially addresses this issue as we match on unconditional turnover. To further address this concern, we follow Seru, Shumway, and Stoffman (2010) and use a hazard model with time-varying covariates to estimate the probability that an investor sells a stock before and after the salience shock.
We estimate the difference-in-differences model in4using a hazard regression (Clotfelter et al. (2008)). The coefficient β3captures the log change in the hazard ratio of selling winners to losers after the shock for noninternet investors;(β3+β5)provides the analogous measure for internet investors. Thus,eβ5measures the relevant difference in differences. Column (1) of TableVreports results from the regression using only internet investors from our main branch. The coefficient onGainis significantly positive, and indicates that internet investors are e0.688≈ 1.98 times more likely to sell a winning stock compared to a losing stock. Column (2) shows that noninternet investors from our main branch are e0.851≈ 2.34 times more likely to sell a winner compared to a loser. We see that the key triple interaction in columns (3) and (6) is significantly positive, which is consistent with results from the previous sections and indicates that there is a significant increase in the disposition effect for internet investors relative to the control group. In TableIA.VIIand FigureIA.3of the Internet Appendix, we show that our propensity score matching and multiple placebo test results are also robust to estimating the disposition effect using hazard regressions.
article.III. Individual Differences in Trading B:
In the previous section, we showed that there is a significant change in theaverageinternet investor's trading behavior following the salience shock. However, previous research shows that there is a large amount of heterogeneity in the disposition effect across investors (Dhar and Zhu (2006)), in which case, our results may be driven by a small number of investors with large portfolios. For example, if investor A holds ten stocks on daytand investor B holds two stocks on dayt, investor A will have five times as many stock-day observations, and will be weighted more heavily when estimating the average disposition effect. Because our data enable us to observe trades at the investor-stock-day level, we can investigate heterogeneity in the size of the disposition effect across investors and test whether our main result is robust to accounting for this heterogeneity.
For each investor, this yields two pairs of coefficients,(αi,pre,γi,pre)and(αi,post,γi,post), with the individual-level disposition effects given byDEi,pre=γi,preandDEi,post=γi,post. To be included in the analysis, we require that, in both the preperiod and the postperiod, each investor have at least 50 stock-day observations in which he can sell a winning stock and at least 50 stock-day observations in which he can sell a losing stock. This sample restriction is nontrivial and only 39% of investors remain but nonetheless is necessary to provide meaningful individual-level estimates.
In Figure6, we plotDEpreversusDEpostfor each of the two groups of investors. If an individual investor's disposition effect does not change over time, we expect the data to lie on the black 45-degree line. Two basic patterns are worth noting from Figure6. First, in both panels the data are concentrated in the first quadrant, indicating that most investors exhibit a positive disposition effect in both the preperiod and the postperiod. Second, in both panels, there is a strong positive relationship between the two variables, indicating that the disposition effect is a persistent behavior. The main question we are interested in is whether(DEi,post−DEi,pre)is larger on average for internet investors compared to noninternet investors. Graphically, this amounts to testing whether the degree to which the data lie above the 45-degree line is larger for internet investors compared to noninternet investors.
Figure6demonstrates that there is substantial variation in the disposition effect, but it also shows that there is substantial heterogeneity across investors in the response to the salience shock. In other words, when the capital gain becomes more salient, some investors exhibit a strong increase in the disposition effect, while others exhibit little change in behavior. This can be seen by the variation in the distance between each point and the 45-degree line.
Here, we explore whether a distinct, but potentially related, trading bias can explain part of this variation. Documenting a correlation between a previously documented trading bias and the new effect that we estimate here is useful because it can provide evidence on the source of the new effect. The previously documented trading bias that we focus on is the “rank effect” (Hartzmark (2015)), which refers to the fact that investors have a greater tendency to sell their best and worst positions when ranked by holding-period returns. Interestingly, this effect also holds when stocks are ranked by alphabetical order of company name: Investors are more likely to sell their first and last positions when stocks in their portfolio are sorted alphabetically.
We are able to estimate the rank effect for 1,376 investors in the preperiod. The mean (median) rank effect is 4.687% (4.976%), which indicates that the probability of selling the highest or lowest ranked stock is 4.687% higher than the probability of selling a nonextreme-ranked stock (a stock that does not exhibit the highest or lowest return in the portfolio). The average rank effect is highly statistically significant with at-statistic of 7.70 and a Wilcoxonp-value lower than 0.001. We next perform a median-split on this sample of 1,376 investors and classify those investors who have a rank effect below 4.976% (the median) as the low-rank effect group; the remaining 688 investors are classified as the high-rank effect group.
We interpret investors in the high-rank effect group as less sophisticated traders and more sensitive to changes in the salience of information. We therefore hypothesize that investors in the high-rank effect group exhibit a larger change in their disposition effect after the salience shock, compared to those investors in the low-rank effect group. To test this prediction, we use the individual-level preperiod and postperiod estimates of the disposition effect from equation5for each of the 1,376 investors for whom we were able to estimate the rank effect. TableVI, Panel B, provides a formal test of the difference in differences. For each specification (each column), the change in the disposition effect is significantly greater for investors in the high-rank effect group compared to the low-rank effect group, as indicated by the significant coefficient on the triple interaction term.
One potential concern with this analysis is that the rank effect and disposition effect are each estimated as a function of the investor's stock-level holding-period returns. It is therefore possible that the two effects have correlated estimation errors, which could lead to a mechanical correlation between the rank effect and the disposition effect and hence bias the results that we report in TableVI, Panel B, although the direction of such a bias is unclear. To attenuate this concern, we follow Hartzmark (2015) and estimate a modified version of the rank effect that is based on the alphabetical ranking of company name in an investor's portfolio. We reestimate equation7, but the variableExtremei,j,tis now set to one if stockjhas the highest or lowest ranking when sorted alphabetically by company name in investori’s portfolio on dayt, and zero otherwise. The company name is prominently displayed in the second column of the investor's trading platform (see Figure1).14
Importantly, we do not use the stock-level holding-period return when estimating this version of the rank effect, which should reduce any concern about a mechanical correlation between the disposition effect and the rank effect. We find that, on average, investors are more likely to sell stocks at the top or bottom of their portfolio when ranked alphabetically by company name, which is consistent with Hartzmark (2015). Specifically, the mean (median) rank effect is 1.885% (1.586%), which indicates that the probability of selling the highest ranked or lowest ranked stock is 1.885% higher than the probability of selling a nonextreme-ranked stock (i.e., a stock that is not first or last when ranked alphabetically by company name). Consistent with Hartzmark (2015), this version of the rank effect is smaller than the rank effect based on returns, but still highly statistically significant. We also find that the two measures of the rank effect have a positive correlation of 0.364 (p< 0.001), suggesting that they are driven by a similar psychological mechanism.
TableVI, Panel C, shows that variation in this version of the rank effect can explain variation in the change in the disposition effect. While the results are slightly weaker than in TableVI, Panel B, for nearly all columns, the change in the disposition effect is significantly greater for investors in the high-rank effect group compared to the low-rank effect group.15Because the measures we use for the rank effect and the disposition effect can vary with turnover, we reestimate the disposition effect at the individual level using hazard regressions, where we reestimate the rank effect as the ratio of the propensity to sell extreme stocks versus nonextreme stocks. TableVII, Panel A, shows that the increase in the individually estimated disposition effects among internet investors relative to noninternet investors is similar when using a hazard regression. Furthermore, in TableVII, Panels B and C show that the individual estimates of the returns-based and alphabetical-based versions of the rank effect both continue to significantly explain variation in the change in the disposition effect. In summary, the results in this section show that there is substantial heterogeneity in how traders respond to a change in information display, and that, we can explain part of this heterogeneity using an existing trading pattern.
article.IV. Potential Mechanisms:
While our data enable us to establish a causal effect on investor behavior, we are unable to identify the mechanism that generates our results. In this section, we consider several mechanisms that can potentially explain the observed increase in the disposition effect. It is important to emphasize that each of the mechanisms we consider is insufficient to explain the change in trading behavior, and needs to be combined with a theory of the disposition effect. In other words, standard models of investor behavior do not predict a greater willingness to sell winning stocks compared to losing stocks, and thus explaining an amplification of this effect must rely on a theory that can generate this asymmetry in the first place.16
With this in mind, a first potential mechanism that can generate an increase in the disposition effect is exogenous attention allocation. Under this mechanism, attention is exogenously directed toward salient attributes, which then receive more weight in the decision-making process (Fehr and Rangel (2011), Bhatia (2013)). In our setting, the capital gain became more salient, in the sense that it became more visually prominent, which may have led to an exogenous (or “bottom-up”) shift in investor attention toward this attribute on the trading screen. The greater weight that investors place on the capital gain during decision making can then trigger a stronger asymmetry in selling winning and losing stocks.
Second, investors may perceive the change in information display as advice from the brokerage house (Benartzi (2001), Madrian and Shea (2001)). This mechanism is likely to be more relevant for unsophisticated investors, who may be looking to the brokerage house for information on how best to manage a portfolio. Interestingly, this channel may also be useful in explaining our cross-sectional results. Hartzmark (2015) reports evidence that unsophisticated investors from a large discount brokerage in the United States exhibit a stronger rank effect compared to sophisticated investors. In our data, we find that investors with stronger rank effects are the same investors who exhibit a larger trading response after the capital gain is added to the trading screen.
Third, our results may be generated by a bounded rationality or search cost mechanism (Caplin and Dean (2015), Gabaix (2019)). Although information on the capital gain was available prior to the change in information display, to obtain this information investors needed to combine their transaction history and current stock prices. This process of searching for and integrating multiple sources of information can be costly to some investors, who may choose to engage in this process only when the cost is lower than the benefit of using the capital gain in their trading decision. Because the change in information display arguably reduced the search cost, it may have increased the frequency with which investors chose to acquire and trade on the capital gain.
Finally, as shown in Figure1, when the brokerage company added the capital gain variable to the trading screen, it also changed the font color for winning stocks from blue to red. Similar to the exogenous attention mechanism discussed above, this may have increased the salience of thesignof the capital gain, which investors then assigned a higher weight in their decision process (Bazley, Cronqvist, and Mormann (2018)). We note, however, that font color cannot explain our cross-sectional results because we measure the rank effect in the period before the display change when there was no variation in font color.
In summary, our results are likely driven by some combination of the mechanisms discussed above. Our data cannot distinguish between these different sources of behavior, but the connection with the rank effect provides preliminary evidence that may be helpful in future empirical tests of the mechanism.
article.V. Robustness Checks:
In this section, we provide robustness checks for our main result on the treatment effect of the salience shock on the disposition effect.
Recall that our main results in TableIIuse a subset of our sample period to balance the length of the preperiod and postperiod. We now examine whether these results are robust to including the full data sample, from January 2003 through December 2009, that is, we reestimate equation1using the full sample. TableVIIIshows that the coefficient on the key triple interactionGain×Post×Internetis significantly positive, in specifications both with and without control variables. FigureIA.2in the Internet Appendix plots the time series of the disposition effect over this extended sample, and TableIA.Xshows that the triple interaction is also significantly positive when using a hazard model.17
Next, we examine whether our main result is robust to a different specification of the reference point used to compute the disposition effect. In all previous analyses, we define a stock as trading at a gain if its current price exceeds the weighted-average purchase price. However, the break-even price was also added to the display screen of the trading platform at the time of the salience shock. It is therefore important to examine how trading behavior might have been affected by the addition of this variable.
Although the two purchase price definitions lead to the same gain/loss classification in 81.0% of observations, it is useful to examine which of the two reference prices better explains selling behavior. We find that when a position is classified as a gain (loss) by both measures, the daily selling propensity is 7.27% (1.96%); when a position is classified as a gain (loss) based on the weighted-average purchase price but a loss (gain) based on the break-even price, the daily selling propensity is 6.80% (2.56%). These reults suggest that selling behavior is much more sensitive to the sign of the return when computed using the weighted-average purchase price compared to the break-even price.
Nonetheless, we still check whether our main difference-in-differences result is robust to using the break-even price as the reference point. TableIXshows that the triple interaction is robust to using this alternative reference point (TableIA.XIshows that this result also holds when using a hazard regression). Taken together, the above results suggest that the weighted-average purchase price is a more likely candidate for the reference point, but even under an alternative reference point specification, the salience shock still significantly impacts the disposition effect.
Finally, we check the robustness of our results using the Odean (1998) sample criteria. Odean (1998) only considers those investor-days for which there is at least one sale and there are at least two stocks available for sale. In TableX, we reestimate equation1based on the Odean (1998) criteria. We find that our results remain robust to this alternative method of sample selection.18
article.VI. Conclusion:
In this paper, we provide evidence that information display has a causal effect on investor behavior in a high-stakes and natural trading environment. The magnitude of the change in trading behavior is economically large, as we estimate a 17% increase in the size of the disposition effect after the change in information display. Our paper thus provides evidence from the field that investor behavior is affected by information display. While our sample period corresponds to the early 2000s, our results are likely to be more applicable today as there has been a large migration toward making investment decisions on a digital platform (Benartzi and Lehrer (2015)).
In addition, our microdata allow us to document substantial heterogeneity across investors in sensitivity to the change in display. We find that investors who exhibit a stronger trading response to the salience shock also exhibit a larger rank effect (Hartzmark (2015)). These results contribute to a recent empirical literature in behavioral economics and finance that seeks to document correlations between behavioral biases (Barber and Odean (2013), Frydman and Camerer (2016), Stango, Yoong, and Zinman (2017), Chapman et al. (2018), Dean and Ortoleva (2019)). Measuring these correlations is useful because it can distill a long list of empirical facts into a smaller number of principal psychological components. This, in turn, can provide guidance for constructing a more unified model of investor behavior (Barberis (2018)).
Finally, our results provide evidence consistent with an important assumption in several recent models of attention and economic choice (Bordalo, Gennaioli, Shleifer (2012,2013a,2013b), Koszegi and Szeidl (2013)). In these models, attention is endogenously allocated toward the attribute in which the alternative is most different relative to other alternatives. An important assumption that links this endogenous allocation of attention to economic behavior is that those attributes that receive more attention also receive more weight in the decision process. Most of the evidence that supports this assumption has come from laboratory experiments. The results reported here show that this link between attention and economic behavior also holds in a higher-stakes environment outside the laboratory.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
